workflow.name: fr_micro_improvements_only
workflow.purpose: Second-pass ONLY: suggest micro-improvements that clearly improve readability/clarity without changing meaning. This workflow is intended to run AFTER the conservative copyedit workflow, and MUST NOT redo basic proofreading tasks.

non_negotiables.item: Never apply silent edits. All proposed text changes must be suggestions only (track changes).
Anchor comments to the smallest relevant span possible.
Meaning preservation is strict: do not change claims, nuance, scope, chronology, agency, modality, or hedging.
Do not introduce new terminology, new facts, or new interpretations.
Do not alter numbers, dates, citations, footnote content, or bibliographic references unless your change is purely formatting-neutral AND unquestionably safe; otherwise add a comment.
If there is any uncertainty about meaning or author intent, do NOT rephrase; add a comment instead.

allowed_changes.item: Micro rephrasing that increases clarity while preserving meaning (e.g., removing a local ambiguity, improving a clunky construction, fixing a confusing pronoun reference).
Very small syntactic adjustments (e.g., word order, agreement-preserving restructuring) that do not change emphasis or nuance.
Splitting an overly long sentence into two if it improves readability and does not change logical relations.
Light tightening (removing a redundant phrase) ONLY if it does not remove nuance.

explicitly_not_in_scope.item: Do not perform full proofreading or copyediting (spelling/grammar/typos/repetitions/typography/bibliography verification). Assume the conservative pass covers that.
Do not rewrite for style preference, elegance, tone, or rhetorical strength.
Do not “improve” academic positioning (e.g., stronger claims, weaker claims, added caveats) — that changes meaning/stance.
Do not global-harmonize terminology or capitalization in this pass; if you notice an issue, leave a comment.

workflow.decision_rule: Propose a micro-improvement ONLY if you are highly confident it (1) clarifies the passage, (2) preserves meaning exactly, and (3) does not introduce any new ambiguity. Otherwise: add a comment describing the potential improvement and the risk/uncertainty.

how_to_express_suggestions.item: When proposing a rephrase, keep the original terminology and register.
When in doubt between edit vs comment: choose comment.
For each suggestion, include a short rationale focused on clarity (not taste).
